content.full_text:
FPPC

8 47 AM '90

JOSEPH W. DIEHL, JR.
RODERICK A. RODEWALD
ALLEN K. HUTCHINSON

DIEHL & RODEWALD
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
1011 PACIFIC STREET
P.O. BOX 1207
SAN LUIS OBISPO, CALIFORNIA 93406

OF COUNSEL
ANNE M. RUSSELL

TELEPHONE:
(805) 541-1030
TELECOPIER:
(805) 541-6870

July 6, 1990


California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

RE:  Santa Maria Public Airport District/Proposal
     by Real Estate Broker to Act as Exclusive Agent

To Whom It May Concern:

     This firm acts as District Counsel for the Santa Maria
Public Airport District ("District"), which is located in the
County of Santa Barbara.  The District recently received
unsolicited requests from three local real estate brokers, each
proposing to act as the exclusive agent for the District in the
marketing, selling, and leasing of district property.

     In reviewing the proposals, this firm became concerned
about the potential for conflict of interest due to some of the
brokers' dual roles as brokers/developers with regard to
property in the vicinity of the airport.  Any broker selected
most likely would be representing other clients having property
for sale or lease in the vicinity of the airport.  The broker,
either directly or indirectly, might also own property for
sale, lease or development in the vicinity of the airport.  The
broker may be making recommendations to the Board as to
property to be sold or leased and possibly analyzing any offer
that could be brought to the District.  A proposed marketing
program to be paid for jointly by the District and the broker
would most likely aid in the sale of other properties listed
with or owned by the broker.  However, the Board of the
Directors of the District ("Board") is concerned that no broker
would be interested in acting as an exclusive agent if the
broker were subject to the disclosure and disqualification
requirements of the Political Reform Act.  Accordingly, before
acting on the three proposals or issuing a formal Request for
Proposals the District requested that I contact you for an
opinion.

Fair Political Practices Commission
July 6, 1990
Page Three

broker may be involved.  The item is ultimately presented to
the Board.  The General Manager may or may not make a
recommendation on the proposal.  The broker is usually
available to answer questions.

     Any broker under the current situation has neither an
on-going relationship, nor a contractual relationship with the
District (other than the District's unilateral offer of payment
of a commission contained in its written policies, which
usually are incorporated into the final written agreement).  If
the District hires a broker as an exclusive agent, the
relationship will be on-going (for the period of time set forth
in the exclusive listing agreement), and contractual.  Although
the ultimate decision to sell or lease a piece of property is
made by the Board, a broker with an on-going relationship with
the Board may be called upon to make recommendations on
specific properties or specific areas of the airport.

                         DISCUSSION

     We understand from reviewing advice letters sent to us by
Mr. John Wallace of your legal division, that if the broker is
a corporation, the broker will not be considered a consultant,
because the Political Reform Act defines a consultant as a
"natural person" who performs certain actions.  However, if the
District enters into an exclusive listing agreement with a
corporate broker, and the current procedure is followed, would
the individual brokers or real estate agents, or other
principals of the broker who would be bringing offers and/or
recommendations to the District for the sale or lease of
district property be considered consultants?  Assuming the
individual brokers/agents/principals ("brokers, etc.") are
considered consultants, does the district staff involvement
under the current procedure constitute "significant intervening
substantive review" thereby insulating the brokers, etc., from
the governmental decision?

     Finally, assuming that the broker, etc., would be a
consultant, and that the current procedure does not constitute
"significant intervening substantive review," what changes
could be made to the current procedure that would prevent the
broker from either (1) being considered a consultant or (2)
being considered a consultant participating in a governmental
decision?  Since the Board is considering issuing a formal
request for proposal, any recommendations you could make as to
changes in current procedure or limitations on actions of the
brokers would be greatly appreciated.  Specifically, would any

Fair Political Practices Commission
July 6, 1990
Page Four


of the following procedures be sufficient to preclude a broker
from either being a consultant, or participating in a
governmental decision:

     1.     Limit the role of the broker to submission of an
offer only.  Not allow the broker to participate in
negotiations, recommendations, or other similar involvement
with district staff and/or Board;

     2.     Limit the broker's role to submission of an offer,
participation in negotiations, and other involvement at the
staff level.  Preclude the broker from making any
recommendation to the Board or appearing in front of the Board;

     3.     Allow the broker to present the offer, participate in
negotiations, make a recommendation to the Board, but have
district staff make an independent recommendation to the Board?

     Your prompt attention to this request would be greatly
appreciated.  Please contact me if you need any additional
information.  I have not submitted the proposals by the brokers
because they do not contain any information concerning the role
the broker would play.  They merely deal with compensation of
the broker for consummating a transaction.  Also, since the
Board may issue a Request for Proposals, it did not seem fair
to make public the financial terms of the proposals.

                              Very truly yours,

                              District Counsel

                              DIEHL & RODEWALD

                              [signature]
                              Anne M. Russell

AMR/afd
Enclosures

cc: Dan Hoback

Fair Political Practices Commission
July 6, 1990
Page Two

ISSUES

1. If the District enters into an exclusive listing
agreement with a real estate broker for the sale or lease of
District property, would the real estate broker be considered
"a consultant" under the Political Reform Act ("Act")?

2. Assuming the real estate broker, or at least its key
employee(s), would be considered a "consultant," would the
broker be deemed to be participating in the making of a
governmental decision under the Act?

3. Would District Staff involvement in the decision
making process provide a "significant intervening substantial
review" of the broker's recommendations (to accept an offer,
enter into a transaction) so as to insulate the broker from the
governmental decision, thereby exempting the broker from the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act?

BACKGROUND

A. District Property Involved
The Santa Maria Public Airport District currently has
several parcels of land for sale in an existing industrial
park. The District also has prepared a Specific Plan for
development of a 738 acre research park, with plans to develop
the park and lease the property on a long term basis. In
addition, the District leases substantial portions of its
improved property, such as hangars, buildings, parking areas,
ramp areas, and inactive runways, as well as most of its
unimproved land for agricultural and grazing purposes.

B. District's Current Policies And Procedures Without An
Exclusive Agent
At present, the District has adopted policies concerning
the payment of commissions for the sale of district property or
the procurement of a long-term (twenty years or longer) lease.
Copies of these policies are enclosed. District property
currently for sale is not listed with any broker. Any broker
or interested individual may submit an offer on any district
property to the District's General Manager. The General Manager
reviews the offer and may either submit the offer directly to the Board, or may enter into negotiations with the
party making the offer. If a broker is involved, the broker
usually participates in the negotiations. The District's
District Counsel reviews the offer and may draft a purchase
agreement or lease. There may be additional negotiations on
the language of any lease or purchase agreement, in which the

sections.facts:
A. District Property Involved
The Santa Maria Public Airport District currently has several parcels of land for sale in an existing industrial park. The District also has prepared a Specific Plan for development of a 738 acre research park, with plans to develop the park and lease the property on a long term basis. In addition, the District leases substantial portions of its improved property, such as hangars, buildings, parking areas, ramp areas, and inactive runways, as well as most of its unimproved land for agricultural and grazing purposes.

B. District’s Current Policies And Procedures Without An Exclusive Agent
At present, the District has adopted policies concerning the payment of commissions for the sale of district property or the procurement of a long-term (twenty years or longer) lease. Copies of these policies are enclosed. District property currently for sale is not listed with any broker. Any broker or interested individual may submit an offer on any district property to the District’s General Manager. The General Manager reviews the offer and may either submit the offer directly to the Board, or may enter into negotiations with the party making the offer. If a broker is involved, the broker usually participates in the negotiations. The District’s District Counsel reviews the offer and may draft a purchase agreement or lease. There may be additional negotiations on the language of any lease or purchase agreement, in which the

We understand from reviewing advice letters sent to us by Mr. John Wallace of your legal division, that if the broker is a corporation, the broker will not be considered a consultant, because the Political Reform Act defines a consultant as a "natural person" who performs certain actions. However, if the District enters into an exclusive listing agreement with a corporate broker, and the current procedure is followed, would the individual brokers or real estate agents, or other principals of the broker who would be bringing offers and/or recommendations to the District for the sale or lease of district property be considered consultants? Assuming the individual brokers/agents/principals ("brokers, etc.") are considered consultants, does the district staff involvement under the current procedure constitute "significant intervening substantive review" thereby insulating the brokers, etc., from the governmental decision?

Finally, assuming that the broker, etc., would be a consultant, and that the current procedure does not constitute "significant intervening substantive review," what changes could be made to the current procedure that would prevent the broker from either (1) being considered a consultant or (2) being considered a consultant participating in a governmental decision? Since the Board is considering issuing a formal request for proposal, any recommendations you could make as to changes in current procedure or limitations on actions of the brokers would be greatly appreciated. Specifically, would any

Fair Political Practices Commission  
July 6, 1990  
Page Four

of the following procedures be sufficient to preclude a broker from either being a consultant, or participating in a governmental decision:

1. Limit the role of the broker to submission of an offer only. Not allow the broker to participate in negotiations, recommendations, or other similar involvement with district staff and/or Board;

2. Limit the broker’s role to submission of an offer, participation in negotiations, and other involvement at the staff level. Preclude the broker from making any recommendation to the Board or appearing in front of the Board;

3. Allow the broker to present the offer, participate in negotiations, make a recommendation to the Board, but have district staff make an independent recommendation to the Board?

Your prompt attention to this request would be greatly appreciated. Please contact me if you need any additional information. I have not submitted the proposals by the brokers because they do not contain any information concerning the role the broker would play. They merely deal with compensation of the broker for consummating a transaction. Also, since the Board may issue a Request for Proposals, it did not seem fair to make public the financial terms of the proposals.
